title: Essential Pharmacy Phrases in Spanish & French Creole

content.text: Speaking Their Language at the Pharmacy Counter

content.body: You do not need to be fluent in another language to provide safe, effective pharmaceutical care across a language barrier. A core set of pharmacy-specific phrases — covering dosing, timing, route, warnings, and verification — can bridge the most critical communication gaps. This lesson provides those phrases in Spanish and Haitian Creole, the two most common non-English languages encountered in Caribbean pharmacy practice.

rows: Take one tablet in the morning
Tome una pastilla en la mañana
Tome una pastilla en la noche
Pran li lè vant ou vid
Are you taking any other medications?
Èske w ap pran lòt medikaman?
Èske w ansent oswa w ap bay tete?
If symptoms worsen, see a doctor
Si los síntomas empeoran, consulte a un médico
Si sentòm yo vin pi grav, ale wè yon doktè
content.caption: Essential Pharmacy Phrases: English, Spanish & Haitian Creole

content.body: Spanish: Most words are pronounced as written. Emphasis falls on the second-to-last syllable unless there is an accent mark. 'Pastilla' = pas-TEE-ya. 'Mañana' = man-YAH-na. Haitian Creole: Pronounced largely phonetically. 'Pran' = 'pran' (take). 'Manje' = 'man-jay' (food). 'Aswè' = 'ah-SWEH' (evening). Practice these phrases until they flow naturally.
content.title: Pronunciation Tips

content.definition: A word that looks or sounds similar in two languages but has a different meaning. In pharmacy, false friends are clinically dangerous — e.g., English 'once' (one time) vs. Spanish 'once' (eleven). Pharmacy technicians working across languages must be aware of common false friends to prevent dosing errors.

rows: Take one tablet twice a day
Tek one tablet two time a day
Clear and direct — most Jamaican patients understand this phrasing
Do not stop taking this medication without asking the doctor
Doh stop tek di medicine widout yuh ask di doctor
Come back if you feel worse
Come back if yuh feel more sick
This medication is for your blood pressure
Dis medicine is fi yuh pressure
'Pressure' is universally understood for hypertension in Jamaica
Can you tell me how you will take this?
Tell mi how yuh ago tek dis when yuh reach home
content.caption: Common Pharmacy Phrases in Jamaican Patois

content.body: Learning even a handful of these phrases demonstrates respect for the patient's linguistic identity and builds trust. Patients who feel their language is respected are more likely to ask questions, disclose concerns, and adhere to their medication regimens.

options: Take one tablet in the morning
Keep the medication in the refrigerator
content.question: The Haitian Creole phrase 'Pran yon grenn nan maten' means:
content.explanation: 'Pran' = take, 'yon grenn' = one tablet/pill, 'nan maten' = in the morning. Therefore, 'Pran yon grenn nan maten' means 'Take one tablet in the morning.' This is one of the most essential pharmacy phrases for serving Haitian Creole-speaking patients.
